title: Equations of Motion for an Economy: Capital Deepening, Technology, and Firm Survival
abstract: We derive equations of motion for capital deepening in a competitive economy directly from accounting identities, without assuming a production function. A profit imperative $η^* \equiv (w/κ+ 1/τ)/(1-f_p)$ sets the minimum viable capital productivity, where $η= Y/K$ [yr$^{-1}$] is capital productivity, $κ= K/L$ is capital per worker, $w$ is the wage rate, $τ$ is the capital lifetime, and $f_p$ is the production tax share. Four coupled relaxation equations govern $κ$, $η$, the frontier productivity $η_{\rm new}$ of new investment, and the labor share $q \equiv w/y$, with the sandwich constraint $η^* \leq η_{\rm new} \leq η$ maintained as an exact invariant. The frontier equation separates two physically distinct channels: a structural cheapening channel ($μ$, always active, drives $η_{\rm new}$ downward) and a productivity channel ($φ$, historically zero). Calibration against BEA 2-digit NAICS sector data (1998--2023) confirms $φ= 0$ for all identifiable sectors over 25 years; the 75-year postwar record extends this finding across four capital lifetimes. A step $φ= 0.01$\,yr$^{-1}$ -- a 1\%/yr improvement in new-capital productivity, modest but historically unprecedented -- nearly doubles the aggregate growth rate within one capital lifetime, a falsifiable prediction with a precise observable signature: upward-curving $η(t)$ in BEA sector data. Firms near the zero-profit threshold have a cash martingale, predicting establishment exit rate $\sim t^{-1/2}$; convolved with the Zipf firm-size distribution~\cite{WP}, this yields firm exit rate $\sim t^{-1/2}\!\log t$ with apparent exponent $b = 0.295 \pm 0.03$, confirmed against BDS data with no free parameters.
summary: This paper develops a dynamical model of economic growth based on accounting identities rather than traditional production functions. It identifies two distinct channels for capital productivity: one that makes capital cheaper and one that makes it more capable. By analyzing decades of US economic data, the author demonstrates that the productivity channel has been stagnant (zero) for the last 75 years. The paper concludes with a specific, testable prediction that a small increase in the productivity channel could nearly double the aggregate growth rate.
main_problem: The paper seeks to derive the equations of motion for economic growth and firm survival without assuming a production function, specifically distinguishing between capital deepening and productivity improvement.
main_result: The study finds that historical growth has been driven by a 'structural cheapening' channel (mu) rather than a 'productivity' channel (phi), which has been zero for 75 years, and predicts that a small positive phi would significantly accelerate growth.
method: The author uses a dynamical systems approach with four coupled ordinary differential equations, calibrated against BEA and BDS economic datasets using the Nelder-Mead algorithm and Kalman filtering.
model_or_system: A competitive economy modeled at the sector level as a dynamical system of coupled relaxation equations governing capital per worker, capital productivity, and labor share, linked to micro-scale firm dynamics via a cash martingale and Zipf's law.
key_observables: Real output per worker (y), capital per worker (kappa), capital productivity (eta), and firm exit rates.
important_parameters: mu (structural cheapening rate), phi (productivity improvement rate), eta* (profit imperative/breakeven productivity), and alpha (Zipf exponent).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a competitive economy, uses accounting identities instead of production functions, and treats firm cash reserves as a martingale under i.i.d. shocks.
figures_summary: Figure 2 provides a forecast of output per worker, comparing historical BEA trends and a business-as-usual projection against a testable prediction of increased growth if the productivity channel (phi) becomes positive.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the derivation of equations of motion from accounting identities, introduces the two-channel productivity model, presents calibration results against historical US sector data, connects macro-dynamics to micro-scale firm exit rates, and concludes with a falsifiable growth prediction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Capital deepening drives output growth. BEA 2-digit NAICS, 1998–2023 (2020 $), 14 active sectors, L-weighted; Real Estate excluded (imputed rent artefact). (a) Real output per worker y = ηκ by sector (log scale); colours by growth rate: blue < 1.25% yr−1, green 1.25–1.75%, crimson > 1.75%; black = L-weighted aggregate (+1.9% yr−1). (b) Growth decomposition ˙y/y = ˙η/η + ˙κ/κ; open markers: non-active sectors. All sectors lie above ˙y/y = 0; capital deepening dominates; ˙η/η ≤0 in 13 of 17 sectors.

FIG. 2. Falsifiable prediction: ϕ = 0.01 yr−1 at 2027, all 14 active sectors. Black dots: BEA observed y(t), 1998– 2023. Grey dashed: historical trend (+1.9% yr−1). Blue: business-as-usual (+1.5% yr−1). Red dash-dot: ϕ = 0.01 yr−1
